title: Shepherd's Pie

subs.name: chickpeas (canned, 400 g, drained) or soya chunks (200 g, soaked) for a vegetarian version
subs.notes: mash the chickpeas lightly to help the filling bind

subs.name: 2 ripe tomatoes, blended and cooked down — gives a fresher, slightly lighter flavour
subs.notes: cook for 5 minutes until the raw smell disappears

subs.name: ghee — makes the mash richer with a more pronounced flavour
subs.notes: use the same quantity; the mash will be slightly more fragrant

subs.name: coconut milk — gives a subtle sweetness and makes the dish dairy-free
subs.notes: use 0.25 cup as it is richer than dairy milk

steps.text: Peel and roughly chop the potatoes. Cover with salted cold water in a saucepan and bring to a boil. Cook for 18–20 minutes until completely tender — a skewer should pass through with no resistance. Drain well, then mash with the butter, warm milk, salt and pepper until very smooth and fluffy. Set aside.

While the potatoes cook, heat a little oil in a wide kadai or heavy pan on high flame. Add the keema and cook, breaking it up with a spoon, for 6–8 minutes until well browned with no pink remaining. Good colour on the meat is what gives the filling depth.

Reduce the heat to medium. Push the browned keema to one side and add the onion, carrot and garlic to the cleared space. Sauté for 5 minutes until the vegetables begin to soften, then stir everything together.

Stir in the tomato paste and cook for 2 minutes until it darkens slightly. Pour in the stock and add the thyme and rosemary sprigs. Season with salt and pepper, bring to a simmer, cover and cook for 15 minutes until the carrots are tender and the sauce has reduced to a thick, scoopable consistency.
Stir in the peas, taste for seasoning, and discard the herb stalks. Preheat your oven to 200°C. Transfer the filling to a large baking dish (about 25×18 cm) and spread it into an even layer.

Spoon the mashed potato on top of the filling and spread it to cover completely, going right to the edges so no steam can escape. Use a fork to create ridges across the surface — these will brown and crisp beautifully in the oven.

Bake for 20–25 minutes until the mash top is golden-brown and the filling is bubbling at the edges. Rest 5 minutes before scooping and serving — the filling sets slightly and the portions hold together better.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: keema → chickpeas (canned, 400 g, drained) or soya chunks (200 g, soaked) for a vegetarian version (mash the chickpeas lightly to help the filling bind); butter → ghee — makes the mash richer with a more pronounced flavour (use the same quantity; the mash will be slightly more fragrant); milk → coconut milk — gives a subtle sweetness and makes the dish dairy-free (use 0.25 cup as it is richer than dairy milk); tomato-paste → 2 ripe tomatoes, blended and cooked down — gives a fresher, slightly lighter flavour (cook for 5 minutes until the raw smell disappears)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Mutton keema is ideal and widely available in India — it suits the longer braising time and has excellent flavour. Use a pressure cooker for the filling: after browning the keema and adding the stock, cook for 2 whistles on medium, then open and reduce the sauce for 3 minutes. Ghee in the mash gives a richer result than butter. If you have no oven for the final step, spread the mash on top and cook covered on very low flame for 15 minutes, then remove the lid and increase heat for 2 minutes to form a light crust.
